title: Hot Take - Legal AI Vendors Are Lying About Accuracy
subject: Hot Take: Every legal AI vendor is lying about accuracy. Yes, all of them.
preheader: The benchmarks are fake. The test sets are cherry-picked. Let's talk about it.
bodyHtml:
LegalTech AI Hub

Hot Take

Every Legal AI Vendor Is Lying About Accuracy. Yes, All of Them.

I’ve reviewed over 60 legal AI products in the past year. Every single one claims an accuracy rate above 90%. Most claim 95%+. A few claim 99%. And I’m here to tell you that these numbers are, at best, meaningless, and at worst, deliberately misleading.

Here’s why.

Problem 1: Nobody agrees on what “accuracy” means. When a contract review tool says it’s 97% accurate, accurate at what? Identifying that a clause exists? Correctly classifying the clause type? Understanding the legal implications of the clause? These are wildly different tasks with wildly different difficulty levels. A tool that’s 97% accurate at spotting indemnification clauses might be 60% accurate at determining whether that indemnification clause actually protects the client. But the marketing page just says “97% accurate.”

Problem 2: The test sets are cherry-picked. Every vendor tests on their own dataset. They choose the documents. They define the ground truth. They run the evaluation. This is like a student writing their own exam and grading their own answers. When independent researchers test these tools on diverse, real-world document sets, accuracy drops 15-25 percentage points. Consistently.

Problem 3: Edge cases are where accuracy matters most. The 95% of contract clauses that are standard and well-formatted? Any tool handles those. The 5% that are unusual, poorly drafted, or deliberately ambiguous? That’s where you actually need help, and that’s exactly where these tools fail. A tool that’s 99% accurate on standard clauses and 40% accurate on edge cases is a tool that fails precisely when you need it most.

Problem 4: There’s no independent testing body. The pharmaceutical industry has the FDA. Financial products have regulatory oversight. Legal AI has nothing. No independent testing. No required disclosure of methodology. No standard benchmarks. Vendors can claim whatever they want, and nobody is checking.

What should you do?

First, ignore accuracy claims entirely. They’re marketing, not data. Second, run your own tests. Take 20 documents you’ve already reviewed manually and run them through any tool you’re evaluating. Compare the outputs. Third, demand methodology. Ask vendors: What dataset did you test on? How was ground truth defined? Who did the evaluation? If they can’t answer clearly, walk away.

The legal AI industry needs an independent testing standard. Until we get one, every accuracy number you see is just a vendor’s best guess at what will sound impressive enough to close the sale.

I’m not saying these tools aren’t useful. Many of them are. I’m saying the accuracy numbers on the tin are fiction, and we should stop pretending otherwise.

DISAGREE?

If you’re a vendor and you think your accuracy claims are legitimate, I’ll publish your rebuttal. Send it over. I’ll also publish the methodology alongside it so readers can judge for themselves. Hit reply.

We review tools honestly at LegalTech AI Hub. Read our independent reviews — no vendor pays for placement.
